summary: 1812-1815 conflict between the United States and the United Kingdom (with British Canadian militias and Native allies), named for the year it began. Causes included the Orders in Council, impressment of American sailors (Chesapeake-Leopard Affair), and the Embargo Act of 1807. Fought across the Canadian/Niagara frontier, the Great Lakes and Atlantic, and the Gulf Coast, with major moments including the burning of Washington, D.C., the defense of Fort McHenry (inspiring "The Star-Spangled Banner"), and the Battle of New Orleans, which was fought after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the war.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the war is identified in giveaways: a conflict between the United States and the United Kingdom (with Upper/Lower Canada and Native allies on the British side) named for the year it began, ended by the Treaty of Ghent, and remembered for the burning of Washington and the Battle of New Orleans.

clues.clue: War between the United States and the United Kingdom named for the year in which it began
examples: name this conflict which saw battles at Lundy's Lane and Lake Erie, as well as the burning of Washington, a war between the United States and the United Kingdom named for the year in which it began.
The burning of Washington, D.C. occurred during this conflict between the United States and Great Britain.

clues.clue: American invasions of Canada were repelled by Tecumseh and British/Canadian forces
examples: The Battle of Lundy's Lane was fought during, for 10 points, what war during which American invasions of Canada were repelled by Tecumseh?

works.name: Causes & Background
works.description: The path to war combined maritime grievances against Britain — impressment of American sailors and trade restriction under the Orders in Council — with frontier tensions after the Battle of Tippecanoe pushed Tecumseh's confederacy toward alliance with the British.

clues.clue: Caused partly by the British Orders in Council restricting neutral trade
examples: Unrest leading to this war was partly caused by the Orders in Council, and before it, one side gained allies due to a raid on Prophetstown.

clues.clue: A raid on Prophetstown (the Battle of Tippecanoe) pushed Tecumseh's confederacy to ally with the British before the war began
examples: before it, one side gained allies due to a raid on Prophetstown.

clues.clue: The Chesapeake-Leopard Affair, involving the impressment of American sailors, was a direct cause; Jefferson's 1807 Embargo Act (limiting trade with France and Britain) followed it
examples: The Chesapeake-Leopard Affair involved the impressment of American sailors, which was a cause of this war.

works.name: Battle of Queenston Heights
works.description: An October 1812 battle on the Niagara frontier in which British general Isaac Brock was killed early on but his forces, under Roger Hale Sheaffe, went on to defeat the American army under Stephen Van Rensselaer, thwarting an attempt to establish a foothold on the Canadian side of the Niagara River.

clues.clue: Forces under Roger Hale Sheaffe defeated Stephen Van Rensselaer's army; Winfield Scott and William Wadsworth also fought on the American side
examples: Captain Runchey's Company of Coloured Men was a militia company that served in a battle during this war, in which forces under Roger Hale Sheaffe defeated Stephen Van Rensselaer.
Name this family of a militia officer who, alongside Winfield Scott and William Wadsworth, fought against the UK and Upper Canada at the Battle of Queenston Heights.

clues.clue: British general Isaac Brock was killed there; the modern Citadelle of Quebec was built afterward
examples: The modern Citadelle of Quebec was constructed following this conflict, during which Isaac Brock was killed at the Battle of Queenstown Heights.
It is best remembered for being the site of British General Isaac Brock's death.

clues.clue: An October 1812 British victory that thwarted an American attempt to establish a base on the Canadian side of the Niagara River
examples: This October 1812 battle was a British victory that thwarted an American attempt to establish a base on the Canadian side of the Niagara River.

works.name: Battle of Beaver Dams (Laura Secord)
works.description: Laura Secord's famous 20-mile walk to warn British lieutenant James FitzGibbon of an impending American attack, allowing the British and their Kahnawà:ke allies to ambush and defeat the Americans near present-day Thorold, Ontario.

clues.clue: Laura Secord walked twenty miles to warn James FitzGibbon of the impending attack
examples: During this conflict, Laura Secord walked twenty miles to warn James FitzGibbon of the impending Battle of Beaver Dams.
The so-called "Paul Revere" of this war, Laura Secord, warned the British that the Americans were coming after hiking 20 miles through swamps.

clues.clue: Secord rushed to help her brother James at the earlier Battle of Queenston Heights before her famous walk
examples: Secord rushed to help her brother James at the Battle of Queenston Heights, fought along a river with this name.

clues.clue: An attack there failed because the British had been warned by Secord
examples: an attack at Beaver Dams failed due to the British being warned by Laura Secord.

works.name: Detroit & Northwest Frontier
works.description: The Michigan/Old Northwest theater, where William Hull's failed invasion of Canada ended in the surrender of Fort Detroit to Isaac Brock and Tecumseh, and a string of smaller frontier engagements (River Raisin, Brownstown, Malcolm's Mills, Prairie du Chien) followed before Tecumseh's death at the Battle of the Thames.

clues.clue: Isaac Brock captured Fort Detroit with help from Tecumseh, after William Hull's failed invasion of Canada led to Hull's surrender and court-martial
examples: name this war during which Isaac Brock captured Fort Detroit with help from Tecumseh.
name this war that included William Hull's failed expedition to British-controlled Canada.

clues.clue: Richard Mentor Johnson claimed to have personally killed Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames
examples: Richard Mentor Johnson claimed to have killed Tecumseh during this war's Battle of the Thames.

clues.clue: Hull's surrender led to his court-martial and connects to the battle at the River Raisin
examples: Another surrender led to the court-martial of William Hull and the battle at the River Raisin.

clues.clue: Thomas Van Horne's forces were ambushed at Sindathon's Village; the ensuing Battle of the Oakwoods saw general Adam Muir solidify his rout at the Battle of Brownstown
examples: At a place known as Sindathon's Village, the forces of Thomas Van Horne were ambushed in this war; the ensuing Battle of the Oakwoods featured general Adam Muir solidify his rout at the Battle of Brownstown.

clues.clue: Duncan McArthur's raid at the Battle of Malcolm's Mills ended a theatre of the war that also included the siege of Prairie du Chien
examples: Duncan McArthur's raid at the Battle of Malcolm's Mills ended a theatre of this war, which included the siege of Prairie du Chien.

clues.clue: The Potawatomi won the Battle of Fort Dearborn; the British separately took the trading post of Fort Mackinac
examples: Name this people who won the Battle of Fort Dearborn and formed the Three Fires Council with the Ojibwe and Odawa.
The British took the trade post Fort Mackinac in this war, which included the battles of Queenston Heights and Lake Erie.

works.name: Naval War: Frigate Duels, the Lakes, and Privateers
works.description: A series of single-ship frigate duels and decisive fresh-water fleet actions, alongside a large privateering campaign that used over 1,100 letters of marque to raid British shipping.

clues.clue: Oliver Hazard Perry's victory at the Battle of Lake Erie was announced with the note "we have met the enemy and they are ours"; he shifted his flagship under fire from the USS Lawrence to the undamaged USS Niagara, aided by the free black sailor Cyrus Tiffany
examples: name this war, in which the note "we have met the enemy and they are ours" announced Oliver Hazard Perry's victory at the Battle of Lake Erie.
During a battle in this war, the free black sailor Cyrus Tiffany likely repeatedly saved the life of the winner during a critical maneuver.

clues.clue: The USS Constitution defeated HMS Guerriere (James Dacre lost the duel to Isaac Hull)
examples: The defeat of the Guerriere by the Constitution was a significant ship action in this war
James Dacre lost a naval duel in this war to Isaac Hull.

clues.clue: A battle nicknamed the "False Nile" (on Lake Champlain) saw the winning side haul the Saratoga's undamaged port side around with cables against the Confiance's squadron
examples: A battle in this war was called the "False Nile" for the tactics used on the Confiance's squadron.
The winning side at that battle won by hauling the Saratoga's undamaged port side around with cables.

clues.clue: David Porter's scheme to attack Pacific commerce mainly entangled him in the local politics of Nuku Hiva
examples: David Porter's absurd scheme to attack Pacific commerce in this war mainly just entangled him in the local politics of Nuku Hiva.

clues.clue: Over 1,100 letters of marque were issued to privateers; the Young Teazer terrorized Mahone Bay, and the Rachel was captured at the Battle of La Guaira
examples: Over 1100 letters of marque were issued during this war, in which the Young Teazer terrorized Mahone Bay.

clues.clue: The Blue Lights were sympathizers who warned the enemy about American blockade runners
examples: A group of sympathizers in this war who warned the enemy about blockade runners were called the Blue Lights.

clues.clue: Charles Ball recorded his time in the Chesapeake Bay Flotilla, which fought at the Battle of Bladensburg
examples: Charles Ball recorded his time in the Chesapeake Bay Flotilla during this war, which fought at the Battle of Bladensburg.

works.name: Burning of Washington, D.C.
works.description: British forces under Robert Ross, fresh off their victory at the Battle of Bladensburg, occupied and burned much of the American capital in August 1814. Dolley Madison ordered the Lansdowne portrait of George Washington rescued before fleeing, and the destruction was ended by a sudden freak tornado.

clues.clue: Led by Robert Ross, occurring shortly after his forces won the Battle of Bladensburg (the retreat there nicknamed the "Bladensburg races")
examples: Name this event led by Robert Ross that took place shortly after Ross's forces won the Battle of Bladensburg.
occurred after a retreat in the "Bladensburg races."

clues.clue: Dolley Madison ordered the Lansdowne portrait of George Washington rescued before fleeing the White House
examples: During it, the Lansdowne portrait was legendarily saved by a woman.
During this event, Dolly Madison ordered slaves to rescue a portrait of George Washington from the White House.

clues.clue: Ended by a freak tornado; Paul Jennings wrote a memoir recalling the event
examples: Paul Jennings wrote a memoir recalling this event, which was ended by a freak tornado.

clues.clue: Refugees sought shelter in Brookeville for a day before moving to the Octagon House
examples: After this event, people sought refuge in Brookeville for a day before moving to the Octagon House.

clues.clue: A soldier paraded a captured hat on a bayonet; a man reportedly jumped in front of a cannon shouting "Are you Goths or Vandals?" to save the Patent Office; a woman opposed "French John's" plan to booby-trap a house with gunpowder
examples: A soldier paraded a leader's hat around on a bayonet during this event
in order to save a patent office.

clues.clue: Rear Admiral Cockburn reportedly ordered the destruction of all the letter "C" type in a newspaper building so his name could no longer be defamed in print
examples: Rear Admiral Cockburn reportedly ordered the destruction of all the "C" type in a newspaper building, so that his name could no longer be defamed.

clues.clue: British commander Alexander Cochrane tried to recruit enslaved people from the American side by promising them freedom
examples: During it, Alexander Cochrane tried to recruit the slaves of the opposing side by promising them freedom.

works.name: Fort McHenry & "The Star-Spangled Banner"
works.description: The British bombardment of Fort McHenry in the Battle of Baltimore, witnessed overnight by Francis Scott Key, who was inspired to write the poem that became "The Star-Spangled Banner," later set to the tune of "To Anacreon in Heaven."

clues.clue: Francis Scott Key wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner" after witnessing the bombing/siege of Fort McHenry
examples: Francis Scott Key composed "Star-Spangled Banner" during what war that included the burning of Washington?
Name this war during which Francis Scott Key wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner" after the siege of Fort McHenry.

clues.clue: Key's poem was later set to the tune of "Anacreon in Heaven"
examples: A poem later set to the tune of "Anacreon in Heaven" was inspired by this war's bombing of Fort McHenry.

clues.clue: A fugitive slave named William Williams defended the fort alongside Key
examples: A fugitive slave named William Williams defended a fort during this war alongside Francis Scott Key, during which time he wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner."

works.name: Battle of New Orleans
works.description: General Andrew Jackson's decisive January 1815 victory over a British invasion force, fought after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the war; aided by pirate Jean Lafitte's Baratarians and celebrated in contemporary songs about the "Hunters of Kentucky."

clues.clue: Andrew Jackson won the Battle of New Orleans in 1815, fought after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended the war
examples: name this war in which General Andrew Jackson won the Battle of New Orleans in 1815.
The Battle of New Orleans occurred after the Treaty of Ghent had already ended this war between the British and the Americans.

clues.clue: Pirate Jean Lafitte and his brother Pierre, organized on the island of Barataria, were instrumental in the American victory despite Governor William Claiborne's earlier order for Lafitte's capture and Commodore Daniel Patterson's capture of his supporters and ships
examples: Lafitte was instrumental in the American victory at New Orleans
Governor William Claiborne put out an order for his capture, and later, many of his supporters and ships were captured by Commodore Daniel Patterson, but he was eventually pardoned.

clues.clue: British Major-General Edward Pakenham died leading troops alongside Admiral Alexander Cochrane
examples: this British major-general died while leading troops alongside Admiral Alexander Cochrane.

clues.clue: Black regiments fought on both sides; drummer Jordan Bankston Noble of the 7th Army Regiment was assisted by Major D'Aquin's Battalion of Free Men of Color
examples: a soldier named Jordan Bankston Noble began with the 7th Army Regiment as a drummer, assisted in one battle by Major D'Aquin's Battalion of Free Men of Color.
name this war in which Black regiments fought on both sides of the Battle of New Orleans.

clues.clue: A song set to the tune of "Unfortunate Miss Bailey" celebrated the "Hunters of Kentucky" (half a horse, half an alligator) defending the Chalmette Plantation; a "Rumpsey Dumpsey" slogan separately referenced Richard Mentor Johnson
examples: A song about this war that was set to the tune of the "Unfortunate Miss Bailey" notes how the title men were "half a horse" and "half an alligator."

works.name: Home Front & Politics
works.description: The war strained American finances and unity: Treasury Secretary Alexander Dallas financed it largely through deficit spending, while New England Federalist discontent culminated in the Hartford Convention.

clues.clue: The Hartford Convention proposed requiring a two-thirds majority in Congress to admit new states or declare war
examples: A meeting during it called for a two-thirds majority to be necessary in Congress to admit new states or declare war and was the Hartford Convention.

clues.clue: The United States financed the war largely through deficit spending under Treasury Secretary Alexander Dallas
examples: The United States financed this war largely through deficit spending by treasury secretary Alexander Dallas.

clues.clue: William Ladd gave up commerce at the war's onset, becoming a farmer and writing pacifist letters
examples: William Ladd gave up commerce at the onset of this war, instead becoming a farmer and writing pacifist letters.

clues.clue: An expedition struggled to cross the Black Swamp after a militia mutinied over clothing rations
examples: An expedition in this war struggled to cross the Black Swamp after a militia mutinied over clothing rations.

works.name: Treaty of Ghent & Aftermath
works.description: The war ended in a status-quo-ante peace at Ghent, followed by the naval-limiting Rush-Bagot Treaty and a series of Native and Canadian-frontier settlements; the war also displaced formerly enslaved Black Loyalists and Black Refugees to Nova Scotia.

clues.clue: The Treaty of Ghent formally ended the war (though the Battle of New Orleans was fought before news arrived)
examples: This war was concluded by the Treaty of Ghent before its final action was fought.
The Battle of Queenston Heights was a major battle of this war, during which Tecumseh repelled US invasions of Canada. The Treaty of Ghent concluded this war.

clues.clue: The Rush-Bagot Treaty, limiting naval presence on Lake Champlain and the Great Lakes, was signed after the war's conclusion
examples: The Rush-Bagot Treaty was signed after the conclusion of this war.

clues.clue: Groups like the Black Refugees and Black Loyalists were sent to Nova Scotia after fighting for the British
examples: Groups like the Black Refugees and the Black Loyalists were sent to Nova Scotia after fighting for the British in this war

clues.clue: Potawatomi leaders Billy Caldwell and Alexander Robinson negotiated the Treaty of Prairie du Chien in the war's aftermath
examples: In the aftermath of this war, Potawatomi leaders Billy Caldwell and Alexander Robinson negotiated Wisconsin's Treaty of Prairie du Chien.

clues.clue: In Lower Canada, the wartime militia performance of Charles de Salaberry's Voltigeurs was mythologized by the Chateau Clique, boosting that faction's postwar prominence alongside the Family Compact
examples: The success of Charles de Salaberry's Voltigeurs during this war led to a militia myth about it promoted by the Chateau Clique, which grew to prominence in the aftermath of this conflict alongside the Family Compact.

comprehensive_summary: The War of 1812 (1812-1815) was fought between the United States and the United Kingdom, whose side included Upper and Lower Canadian militias and Native allies under Tecumseh. Its causes combined maritime grievances against Britain — impressment of American sailors highlighted by the Chesapeake-Leopard Affair, and trade restrictions under the Orders in Council, which Jefferson's 1807 Embargo Act had already tried to counter — with frontier tension after the Battle of Tippecanoe (a raid on Prophetstown) pushed Tecumseh's confederacy toward alliance with Britain. On the Niagara frontier, British general Isaac Brock was killed winning the Battle of Queenston Heights, where Roger Hale Sheaffe's forces defeated Stephen Van Rensselaer's army (with Winfield Scott and William Wadsworth on the American side); nearby, Laura Secord's twenty-mile walk to warn James FitzGibbon enabled the British victory at the Battle of Beaver Dams. In the Detroit/Northwest theater, William Hull's failed invasion of Canada ended in the surrender of Fort Detroit to Brock and Tecumseh (leading to Hull's court-martial), followed by fighting at the River Raisin, Brownstown, and Malcolm's Mills, and Tecumseh's death at the Battle of the Thames, where Richard Mentor Johnson claimed the kill. At sea and on the Great Lakes, the USS Constitution defeated HMS Guerriere, Oliver Hazard Perry won the Battle of Lake Erie ("we have met the enemy and they are ours," shifting his flag from the Lawrence to the Niagara), and American forces won the "False Nile" battle on Lake Champlain by hauling the Saratoga's undamaged side around with cables; over 1,100 letters of marque also fueled a large privateering campaign. In August 1814, British forces under Robert Ross burned Washington, D.C. after winning the Battle of Bladensburg, an event remembered for Dolley Madison's rescue of the Lansdowne portrait of George Washington and ended by a freak tornado. Weeks later, Francis Scott Key witnessed the British bombardment of Fort McHenry in the Battle of Baltimore and wrote the poem that became "The Star-Spangled Banner." The war strained American unity and finances, prompting Treasury Secretary Alexander Dallas's deficit financing and New England Federalist discontent at the Hartford Convention. The Treaty of Ghent formally ended the war in an effective return to the prewar status quo, but news arrived too late to stop Andrew Jackson's decisive victory at the Battle of New Orleans (January 1815), aided by pirate Jean Lafitte's Baratarians and commemorated in songs about the "Hunters of Kentucky." Postwar settlements included the naval-limiting Rush-Bagot Treaty, the Treaty of Prairie du Chien with the Potawatomi, and the resettlement of Black Loyalists and Black Refugees who had fought for Britain to Nova Scotia, while in Lower Canada a militia myth around Charles de Salaberry's Voltigeurs fed the postwar rise of the Chateau Clique.
recursive_suggestions: Andrew Jackson — already has his own topic page; the Battle of New Orleans section there overlaps with this war's aftermath
Tecumseh — recurring figure across the Detroit frontier and Queenston Heights sections; could merit a standalone Figure topic
Battle of New Orleans — recurring answerline presence (4x tossup/bonus mentions) independent of the war itself; watch for growth into its own topic
Treaty of Ghent — recurring bonus-part answerline (4x); currently folded into this page's Aftermath section
Laura Secord — the 'Paul Revere of the war'; distinct Canadian-history figure with independent clue presence
Napoleon's invasion of Russia / French invasion of Russia (1812) — same calendar year but an unrelated European conflict; filtered out of this analysis, watch for confusion in search results
Jean Lafitte — pirate/privateer with independent bonus-part answerline presence (Barataria, Battle of New Orleans)
Isaac Brock — British general killed at Queenston Heights; recurring across multiple sections here
